vulnerabilities.title: Missing msg.sender Validation in lzCompose Allows Token Theft
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDRouter.lzCompose has no check that msg.sender is the LayerZero Endpoint. Any address can call it with crafted parameters. In LayerZero v2, token delivery (lzReceive) and compose execution (lzCompose) are separate transactions. After lzReceive delivers OFT tokens to the router in block N, the attacker observes the confirmed token balance and submits a malicious lzCompose call in block N+1 with a crafted message setting composeFrom to their address and an invalid composeMsg length (triggering r

description.desc: external payable function lzCompose with no require(msg.sender == endpoint) check; anyone can call with arbitrary _from, _message, _guid parameters

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Every cross-chain minting operation passes through the router. Between lzReceive and lzCompose, the router holds the user's OFT tokens. Any attacker monitoring the chain can steal 100% of in-transit funds. Capital required: zero (only gas for the lzCompose call). The attack is deterministic, not probabilistic - the attacker sees confirmed balances and acts in the next block. There is no defense mechanism in the code.

vulnerabilities.title: lzCompose Permanently Reverts via payable.transfer in _refundToken
vulnerabilities.summary: _refundToken uses payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) which forwards only 2300 gas. If _cantoRefundAddress is a smart contract wallet (Gnosis Safe, AA wallet) or a contract without a receive function, this reverts. Since _refundToken is called from every failure path in lzCompose, and lzCompose MUST NOT revert per LayerZero spec, any revert permanently locks the OFT tokens delivered by LayerZero in the router.

description.desc: payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) — 2300 gas stipend reverts for contract recipients; uncaught revert propagates to lzCompose

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Any user whose refund address is a contract wallet (Gnosis Safe, ERC-4337 wallets, or any contract with non-trivial receive logic) risks permanent loss of their cross-chain transfer amount if any failure condition is triggered in lzCompose. This is not a theoretical concern - smart contract wallets are increasingly common, especially among institutional users and DAOs. On Canto, where the protocol operates, even EIP-3074 or future account abstraction adoption would expand the affected user base. There is no recovery mechanism in the contract for locked tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: lzCompose Reverts via Unwrapped ASDOFT.transfer in Local Send Path
vulnerabilities.summary: In _sendASD when _dstLzEid == cantoLzEID (local Canto transfer), ASDOFT(_cantoAsdAddress).transfer() is called directly without try/catch or low-level call wrapping. If this transfer fails for any reason (insufficient ASD balance in router due to failed mint, paused token, blacklisted receiver), the entire lzCompose reverts, permanently locking the user's deposited NOTE and original OFT tokens.

description.desc: ASDOFT(_payload._cantoAsdAddress).transfer(_payload._dstReceiver, _amount) — direct call not wrapped in try/catch; revert propagates to lzCompose

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - Any user targeting local Canto delivery (dstLzEid == cantoLzEID) is vulnerable. If the ASDOFT.transfer() call reverts for any reason, the user permanently loses their OFT tokens that were already delivered to the router by LayerZero. There is no recovery mechanism (no sweep function, no admin rescue). The router holds 0 tokens after revert (state is rolled back), but the LayerZero message is consumed/failed. The compose message may be retryable through LayerZero's retry mechanism, but if the root cause persists (e.g., the payload specifies an invalid _cantoAsdAddress), every retry will fail identically.

vulnerabilities.title: Inverted Swap Direction in _swapOFTForNote Makes All Swaps Fail
vulnerabilities.summary: In _swapOFTForNote, the `isBuy` parameter to CrocSwap is set to `!isNoteBase`. The intent is to sell asdUSDC for NOTE, but the logic is inverted. When NOTE is base token, isBuy=false means 'sell base (NOTE) for quote (asdUSDC)' — the opposite of what's needed. When NOTE is quote, isBuy=true means 'buy base (asdUSDC) with quote (NOTE)' — also wrong. In both cases, the swap attempts to sell NOTE that the router doesn't hold, causing every swap to fail.

description.desc: isBuy=!isNoteBase passed to calcImpact and swap; correct value should be isNoteBase to buy NOTE with asdUSDC

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL: Complete protocol non-functionality. Every cross-chain mint operation fails at the swap step. Users who send OFT stablecoins expecting ASD tokens instead receive asdUSDC refunds. The core value proposition of the protocol (cross-chain ASD minting) is entirely broken. This affects all users on all chains. A contract redeployment or upgrade would be required to fix the issue.

vulnerabilities.title: SendParam composeMsg Set to String '0x' Instead of Empty Bytes Breaks Cross-Chain ASD Delivery
vulnerabilities.summary: In _sendASD, the cross-chain send path constructs SendParam with composeMsg: '0x' and oftCmd: '0x'. In Solidity, string '0x' converts to bytes [0x30, 0x78] (2 bytes), NOT empty bytes. LayerZero OFT checks composeMsg.length > 0 to determine if compose is requested. Since length == 2, every cross-chain ASD send includes an unintended compose callback with garbage data on the destination chain.

description.desc: composeMsg: '0x', oftCmd: '0x' — string literal '0x' is 2 bytes [0x30, 0x78], not empty bytes('')

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Every cross-chain ASD delivery from the router includes an unintended compose callback. This affects all users who initiate cross-chain operations where the destination is not Canto. Depending on the LayerZero endpoint's handling of compose messages with invalid data, tokens could be delayed, require manual intervention, or be stuck in the endpoint's retry mechanism. The oftCmd being non-empty may also cause unexpected send behavior (e.g., bus mode instead of taxi mode).

vulnerabilities.title: Missing CrocSwap Token Approval Prevents All Swaps
vulnerabilities.summary: The _swapOFTForNote function calls CrocSwap.swap to exchange asdUSDC for NOTE, but never approves CrocSwap to spend the router's asdUSDC tokens. With reserveFlags=0, CrocSwap uses transferFrom to pull tokens, which requires prior ERC20 approval. The router has no function to set this approval, and the deployment script doesn't set it. Every swap call fails.

description.desc: crocSwapAddress.call(abi.encodeWithSelector(ICrocSwapDex.swap.selector, ...)) without prior IERC20(asdUSDC).approve(crocSwapAddress, amount)

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - The primary protocol function (cross-chain stablecoin to ASD conversion) is completely non-functional. All users who send OFT stablecoins expecting ASD will receive asdUSDC refunds instead. The protocol cannot fulfill its core use case without contract redeployment or upgrade (which is not supported by the current non-upgradeable architecture).

vulnerabilities.title: _depositNoteToASDVault Low-Level Call to EOA Silently Succeeds Without Minting ASD
vulnerabilities.summary: _depositNoteToASDVault uses a low-level .call() to invoke mint on _cantoAsdAddress, a user-controlled parameter. Low-level calls to EOAs always return success=true with empty return data. If a user provides an EOA or non-ASD contract as _cantoAsdAddress, the call 'succeeds', NOTE is approved but not consumed (or consumed by a malicious contract), and no ASD is minted to the router. The subsequent _sendASD call then reverts (TECH-H003) or transfers 0 tokens.

description.desc: (bool success, bytes memory errReason) = _asdVault.call(...) — no validation of _asdVault address; EOA calls return (true, '') without executing any code

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. For the EOA case: permanent lzCompose revert locks user's swapped NOTE and native gas tokens in the router. For the malicious contract case: direct theft of NOTE tokens that were swapped from the user's original OFT stablecoins. In both cases, the non-revert guarantee of lzCompose is violated, potentially blocking the LayerZero message channel. The residual NOTE approval to an arbitrary address creates an ongoing risk for any future NOTE that enters the router.

vulnerabilities.title: Bare IERC20.transfer in _refundToken Silently Fails for Non-Standard Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: _refundToken uses IERC20(_tokenAddress).transfer() (bare transfer, not SafeERC20.safeTransfer). Some ERC20 tokens return false on failure instead of reverting, and some (like USDT) return no value at all. Bare .transfer() in Solidity 0.8 will revert on no-return-value tokens (ABI decoding fails expecting bool). For tokens returning false, the transfer silently fails — the refund event is emitted but tokens stay in the router.

description.desc: IERC20(_tokenAddress).transfer(_refundAddress, _amount) — bare transfer without SafeERC20; return value not checked

vulnerabilities.impact: The impact ranges from silent loss of refund amounts (false-return tokens) to permanent locking of all tokens in a compose message (no-return tokens causing lzCompose revert). Since _refundToken is the safety mechanism for ALL failure paths in lzCompose, a defect here means the safety net itself is broken. The absence of a sweep/recovery function in the router means locked tokens are unrecoverable.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Breaks asdUSDC Solvency Invariant
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDUSDC.deposit records usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount using the input amount, not the actual received amount after transfer. If a whitelisted USDC version has fee-on-transfer behavior, the contract receives less than _amount but records the full _amount. This inflates usdcBalances above the real balance, causing insolvency — later withdrawers cannot withdraw because the contract holds insufficient tokens.

description.desc: usdcBalances[_usdcVersion] += _amount after safeTransferFrom; _amount is transfer amount, not received amount

vulnerabilities.impact: If a fee-on-transfer USDC variant is whitelisted: (1) usdcBalances becomes permanently inflated above actual token holdings, (2) early withdrawers can withdraw their full amount at the expense of later withdrawers, (3) the last withdrawer(s) cannot withdraw due to insufficient actual balance, (4) the owner's recover() function cannot fix the discrepancy because it will underflow and revert, (5) the insolvency compounds with each deposit. This directly violates INV-ECON-003: ERC20(v).balanceOf(address(this)) >= usdcBalances[v].

vulnerabilities.title: recover() Missing Whitelist Check Allows Minting asdUSDC Backed by Arbitrary Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDUSDC.recover() does not check whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]. The owner can call recover with any ERC20 token address (including worthless tokens airdropped to the contract), minting asdUSDC backed by non-USDC tokens. This asdUSDC can then be used to withdraw real USDC from other versions' balances.

description.desc: No require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion]) in recover(); deposit() and withdraw() both have this check

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - A compromised or malicious owner can drain all legitimate USDC deposits from the contract. The attack cost is negligible (just the gas for sending a worthless token + calling recover + calling withdraw). All asdUSDC depositors lose their funds with no recovery path. Even without malicious intent, accidental use of recover() with a non-USDC token breaks the backing invariant, diluting all depositors' claims.

vulnerabilities.title: asdUSDC Withdraw Precision Loss Burns Tokens for Zero USDC Return
vulnerabilities.summary: In ASDUSDC.withdraw(), amountToWithdraw = _amount / (10 ** (18 - usdcDecimals)). For 6-decimal USDC, any _amount < 10^12 (less than 1 micro-USDC worth of asdUSDC) results in amountToWithdraw = 0 due to integer truncation. The user's asdUSDC tokens are burned, but they receive 0 USDC. The 'lost' value stays in the contract with no recovery path for the user.

description.desc: amountToWithdraw = _amount / (10 ** (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals())); burns _amount but withdraws truncated value

vulnerabilities.impact: The per-transaction financial impact is small (< $0.000001 for sub-10^12 amounts), but the code violates the fundamental invariant that burning asdUSDC should always return proportional USDC. This is a genuine code defect: the function permits a state where user tokens are destroyed with zero return value. The dust accumulates in the contract over time. The defect also breaks the conservation invariant INV-STATE-009 cited in the hypothesis.

vulnerabilities.title: ASDUSDC decimal underflow/overflow risk when whitelisting tokens with decimals > 18 can brick deposit/withdraw/recover for that token
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDUSDC.deposit/withdraw/recover compute scaling with (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()). If a whitelisted token reports decimals > 18, the subtraction underflows and reverts in Solidity 0.8, permanently breaking deposit/withdraw/recover for that version and potentially trapping that token if it is transferred in.

description.desc: 10 ** (this.decimals() - ERC20(_usdcVersion).decimals()) used in multiple functions; subtraction can underflow if token decimals > 18

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect requires owner misconfiguration to trigger, but once triggered: (1) deposit/withdraw/recover are permanently bricked for the affected token version; (2) tokens sent directly to the contract for that version are unrecoverable; (3) if the ASDRouter interacts with the whitelisted version, the deposit call reverts, causing lzCompose to fail at the deposit step and trigger refunds (which may themselves fail per other identified issues). The owner can mitigate by de-whitelisting the token, but any tokens already in the contract remain trapped.

vulnerabilities.title: Unrevoked Approval Allows Malicious Vault to Drain Router's NOTE
vulnerabilities.summary: `_depositNoteToASDVault` approves a user-provided vault address to spend NOTE, then makes a low-level call to the vault's `mint` selector. If the malicious vault intentionally fails the call, the approval is not revoked. The attacker can later use this leftover allowance to steal any NOTE that accumulates in the router.

description.desc: Approval not reset to 0 if low-level call fails
Affected function: mint

vulnerabilities.impact: Direct impact: theft of any NOTE tokens that accumulate in the router contract. The magnitude depends on whether and how much NOTE accumulates. In normal operation, the router should hold 0 NOTE between calls. However, the use of bare `IERC20.transfer` in `_refundToken` (which doesn't check return values) increases the chance of NOTE getting stuck. The defect also creates a latent risk: any future code change or integration that causes NOTE to transit through the router could be exploitable without the developers realizing the pre-existing approval.

vulnerabilities.title: asdOFT.burn Exchange Rate Manipulation Risk
vulnerabilities.summary: `asdOFT.burn` redeems NOTE from the Canto Lending Market using `cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount)`. It assumes 1 asD = 1 NOTE. However, if the lending market suffers bad debt and the exchange rate drops, redeeming `_amount` of NOTE might burn more cNOTE than expected, accelerating insolvency for remaining asD holders.

description.desc: Redeeming exact underlying amount without checking cToken exchange rate impact

vulnerabilities.impact: If the cNOTE exchange rate decreases: (1) Early burners extract full value at the expense of remaining holders; (2) Late burners lose their NOTE backing entirely; (3) Owner cannot call withdrawCarry due to underflow revert; (4) Total value at risk equals the entire totalSupply of asD tokens. The severity depends on the magnitude of the exchange rate decrease and the speed of the bank run.

vulnerabilities.title: De-whitelisting USDC Version Permanently Locks Deposited Funds
vulnerabilities.summary: If the owner de-whitelists a USDC version via updateWhitelist(_version, false) while usdcBalances[_version] > 0, depositors of that version can no longer withdraw. The withdraw function requires whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion] == true. asdUSDC tokens remain burnable but the underlying USDC is permanently locked. There is no migration path or emergency withdrawal mechanism.

description.desc: require(whitelistedUSDCVersions[_usdcVersion], ...) in withdraw; no check in updateWhitelist that usdcBalances == 0 before de-whitelisting

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium-to-High impact. User funds become temporarily inaccessible upon de-whitelisting. If owner cannot or will not re-whitelist (key loss, renounced ownership, security concern with the USDC version), funds are permanently locked. The asdUSDC tokens remain in circulation but have no withdrawal path for that specific USDC version, breaking the backing invariant for those deposits. Users holding asdUSDC could still withdraw other whitelisted USDC versions if those have sufficient balance, but this drains other depositors' reserves.

vulnerabilities.title: ASDOFT.withdrawCarry() can become permanently unusable if balanceOfUnderlying < totalSupply (underflow revert), blocking operational carry management during lending-market stress
vulnerabilities.summary: withdrawCarry computes maximumWithdrawable = balanceOfUnderlying - totalSupply(). If the backing invariant is ever temporarily violated (e.g., due to lending market loss, rounding, or delayed interest accrual), this subtraction reverts under Solidity 0.8, bricking carry withdrawal entirely. During incidents, inability to withdraw/redistribute carry may break planned operations (e.g., treasury funding).

description.desc: maximumWithdrawable = CTokenInterface(cNote).balanceOfUnderlying(address(this)) - totalSupply();

vulnerabilities.impact: When triggered, the owner cannot call withdrawCarry with any parameter value (including 0, which would set _amount = maximumWithdrawable but the computation of maximumWithdrawable itself reverts first). This blocks all carry management operations. During a lending market stress event — precisely when operational control is most needed — the owner loses the ability to manage protocol yield. Recovery requires either restoring the backing invariant externally (e.g., by depositing more NOTE via mint()) or deploying a new contract. Note that burn() also calls redeemUnderlying and could fail if there's truly insufficient underlying, creating a broader insolvency issue beyond just withdrawCarry.

vulnerabilities.title: Unsafe uint128 Cast in Swap Truncates Large Amounts
vulnerabilities.summary: The _swapOFTForNote function casts the swap _amount from uint256 to uint128 without checking for overflow. Amounts larger than type(uint128).max will be silently truncated.

description.desc: uint128 amountConverted = uint128(_amount)

vulnerabilities.impact: The code contains a genuine defect (unsafe downcast) that violates the principle of safe type conversions. While practically unreachable with standard USDC tokens, the defect means: (1) amounts > uint128.max would be silently truncated, (2) only a fraction of deposited tokens would be swapped, (3) the remainder would be permanently stuck in the router. The severity is correctly assessed as MEDIUM given the extreme preconditions required.

vulnerabilities.title: Excess msg.value from LayerZero Execution is Permanently Locked in Router
vulnerabilities.summary: In the success path of `_sendASD` (cross-chain), the router uses `_payload._feeForSend` for the LayerZero fee. Any excess `msg.value` sent by the executor is left in the router. Because `_refundToken` strictly limits native refunds to the current `msg.value`, these accumulated native tokens can never be recovered or stolen, resulting in a permanent loss.

description.desc: call{value: _payload._feeForSend} leaves msg.value - _feeForSend in contract

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The amount locked per transaction is small (the difference between executor-estimated fees and user-specified _feeForSend), but it accumulates over all successful cross-chain sends. The tokens are genuinely irrecoverable, constituting permanent value destruction. The impact scales with protocol usage volume.

vulnerabilities.title: ASDRouter Deployment Script Missing Constructor Argument and Calling Non-Existent Function
vulnerabilities.summary: The deployASDRouter.js script deploys ASDRouter with 4 arguments (note, lzEndpointEID, crocSwap, crocImpact) but the constructor requires 5 (also needs _asdUSDCAddress). This causes deployment to fail entirely. Additionally, the script calls contract.updateWhitelist() which does not exist on ASDRouter (it exists on ASDUSDC). If somehow deployed with a modified constructor or script, the asdUSDC state variable would be uninitialized (address(0)), causing all deposit calls to ASDUSDC(address(0)).d

description.desc: Constructor takes 5 parameters including _asdUSDCAddress; deployment script passes only 4 arguments omitting asdUSDC address

vulnerabilities.impact: The deployment script cannot deploy ASDRouter as written. This blocks the entire protocol from being deployed via the provided script. If deployed through other means with the correct arguments, the protocol would function. However, the existence of two distinct bugs (missing argument AND calling non-existent function) strongly suggests the script and contract are out of sync, creating operational risk.

vulnerabilities.title: Router can be forced to retain tokens due to non-resetting approvals and partial-step refunds, enabling later draining via crafted flows
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDRouter sets token approvals (IERC20(_from).approve(asdUSDC, amountLD) and IERC20(noteAddress).approve(_asdVault, _amountNote)) and does not reset them to zero on failure paths. Combined with partial-step refunds (refund may be in asdUSDC or NOTE) and multiple revert-prone external calls, it is plausible for the router to end up retaining residual token balances (e.g., due to fee-on-transfer/rounding, swap partial fills, or external protocol behaviors). Any retained balances can then be target

description.desc: approve(asdUSDC, amountLD) and approve(_asdVault, _amountNote) without resetting to 0 on failure; refunds can occur after approvals

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL for the immediate callback vector: any cross-chain compose message can be crafted to steal the NOTE that results from the swap. The attacker uses their own OFT tokens as input, so they sacrifice OFT stablecoins but gain NOTE (which may be profitable depending on exchange rates and the swap mechanics). HIGH for the lingering approval scenario: requires balance accumulation conditions but represents a persistent drain surface.

vulnerabilities.title: Out-of-Bounds Revert in Message Decoding Blocks LayerZero Channel
vulnerabilities.summary: `lzCompose` passes the raw `_message` to `_decodeOFTComposeMsg`. If the message is shorter than required by `OFTComposeMsgCodec`, the decoding will revert with an out-of-bounds error. This violates the non-revert invariant of `lzCompose`, permanently blocking the LayerZero message channel.

description.desc: Unsafe decoding of arbitrary length calldata

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect is real: lzCompose can revert when it must not. Through direct calls (possible due to missing access control - INV-ACCESS-001), this is trivially triggerable but doesn't block the LZ channel. Through the LZ endpoint, practical exploitability is low since the endpoint formats messages correctly. The 'permanently blocking' claim is somewhat overstated for the most likely attack vector, but the invariant violation is genuine. If a malformed message did arrive through the endpoint (e.g., from a buggy/malicious OFT contract on source chain), the compose WOULD be permanently stuck since retries would hit the same revert.

vulnerabilities.title: ambientPoolFor Hardcoded POOL_PARAM_SLOT Creates Silent Pool Validation Failure on Ambient Upgrade
vulnerabilities.summary: The ambientPoolFor function reads a storage slot from CrocSwapDex using hardcoded POOL_PARAM_SLOT = 65545. If Ambient Finance upgrades their contract or changes storage layout, this slot may return stale/incorrect data. A non-zero return for a non-existent pool would bypass the pool existence check, allowing calcImpact and swap to proceed against an invalid or unintended pool. Conversely, a zero return for a valid pool would cause all swaps to fail silently (protocol DoS). Since the swap is wrap

description.desc: uint POOL_PARAM_SLOT = 65545 hardcoded; slot derived via keccak256(abi.encode(poolKey, POOL_PARAM_SLOT)); ICrocSwapDex(crocSwapAddress).readSlot(uint256(slot)) used for pool existence check

vulnerabilities.impact: No current impact. Future impact is contingent on operational decisions (Ambient upgrade without ASDRouter redeployment). If such a scenario occurred, the impact would be either: (a) pool check returns 0 for valid pools → swap fails → users refunded in asdUSDC (graceful degradation via the existing error handling), or (b) pool check returns non-zero for invalid pools → swap attempt on CrocSwapDex would likely fail → users refunded in asdUSDC. In both cases, the low-level call wrapper on the swap (line ~194) catches the failure and triggers a refund.

vulnerabilities.title: ASDOFT.burn Blocked by cNote Lending Market Illiquidity Creates Temporary Fund Lock
vulnerabilities.summary: ASDOFT.burn calls cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount), which can return a non-zero error code if the Canto Lending Market has insufficient liquidity (high utilization rate). The require(returnCode == 0) then reverts, preventing users from burning their asD tokens. In a high-utilization scenario (e.g., heavy borrowing against cNOTE), asD holders are temporarily locked out of redemptions. This creates a bank-run dynamic: as some users fail to redeem, panic increases, and remaining users rush to r

description.desc: uint256 returnCode = cNoteToken.redeemUnderlying(_amount); require(returnCode == 0) - no handling of insufficient liquidity error codes

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. During periods of high cNOTE market utilization (natural or induced), all asD holders are blocked from burning their tokens. The impact is temporary (until utilization decreases) but affects all users simultaneously. This could cause: (1) loss of confidence in asD's 1:1 redeemability, (2) panic selling on secondary markets at a discount, (3) bank-run dynamics where early redeemers succeed and later ones are blocked. The owner's withdrawCarry is also blocked, though this has lower impact. The backing still exists on-paper in cNOTE, but users cannot access their NOTE.
